edHandoff.summary: 42-year-old male presenting with severe epigastric pain radiating to back after heavy alcohol use over the weekend. Lipase 1,840. CT shows peripancreatic inflammation, no necrosis. 2L NS given, morphine 4mg x2. Pain improved to 6/10. Vitals improved but still tachycardic at 98.
edHandoff.disposition: Admit to medicine for IV fluids, pain management, and monitoring

sources.name: AGA Guidelines on Initial Management of Acute Pancreatitis

patient.communicationStyle: Initially vague about alcohol consumption. Becomes more open if asked non-judgmentally. Frustrated about dietary restrictions.
patient.emotionalState: In pain, slightly defensive about alcohol history

patientPhrasing: feels like someone stabbed me in the stomach
pain goes straight through to my back
chiefComplaint.onset: Started 12 hours ago after a weekend of heavy drinking (bachelor party)
chiefComplaint.severity: 9/10 at worst, now 6/10 with IV meds

pmh: One prior episode of pancreatitis (2 years ago, also alcohol-related, managed conservatively)

socialHistory.smoking: Half pack per day for 15 years
socialHistory.alcohol: Reports '4-5 drinks on weekends' — but weekend binge was 15+ drinks over 2 days at bachelor party. Likely underreporting baseline use.

physicalExam.general: Middle-aged male, appears uncomfortable, lying in fetal position, prefers leaning forward
physicalExam.pulmonary: Clear bilaterally. Decreased breath sounds at left base (small reactive pleural effusion possible).
physicalExam.abdominal: Epigastric tenderness with guarding. No rebound. Decreased bowel sounds. No palpable mass. No Grey Turner or Cullen signs.

labs.note: >3x ULN — meets diagnostic criteria for acute pancreatitis
Elevated but less specific than lipase
Elevated — dehydration. BUN >20 is part of BISAP score.
Stress response vs pancreatic endocrine dysfunction
Mildly low — saponification, monitor closely (Ranson's criterion)
Mildly elevated but <1,000 — not the cause

imaging.findings: No gallstones. No biliary dilation. Common bile duct 4mm. Gallbladder wall normal.

Diffuse pancreatic enlargement with peripancreatic fat stranding and small amount of peripancreatic fluid. No pancreatic necrosis (uniform enhancement). Small left pleural effusion. No pseudocyst or abscess.
imaging.impression: Acute interstitial pancreatitis without necrosis. Peripancreatic inflammatory changes.

admittingOrders.diet: NPO initially. Advance to low-fat solid diet when pain improving (within 24 hours if tolerated — early feeding is preferred over prolonged NPO).
admittingOrders.ivFluids: Lactated Ringer's at 200-250mL/hr for initial 12-24 hours (goal-directed — target urine output >0.5mL/kg/hr, BUN trending down, HR normalizing)
admittingOrders.painManagement: Hydromorphone 0.5-1mg IV q3h PRN (avoid meperidine — no evidence it's superior; acetaminophen 1g IV/PO q6h scheduled as adjunct)
admittingOrders.monitoring: Vitals q4h. Strict I&O. Foley only if unable to void. Trend lipase (does NOT need to normalize before advancing diet). BMP q12h x 48 hours. CBC daily.
admittingOrders.dvtProphylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SQ daily or SCDs if contraindicated
admittingOrders.consultations: GI consult if clinical worsening, suspected necrosis, or need for ERCP. Social work/addiction counseling for alcohol use.

keyLearningPoints: Diagnosis requires 2 of 3: characteristic abdominal pain, lipase >3x ULN, imaging findings
Severity scoring: BISAP (BUN>25, impaired mental status, SIRS, age>60, pleural effusion) — this patient scores 2 (BUN, pleural effusion)
LR is preferred over NS for fluid resuscitation (less hyperchloremic acidosis, may reduce SIRS)
Early feeding (within 24 hours) is superior to prolonged NPO — advances recovery
Gallstone pancreatitis is excluded by normal RUQ US and normal ALT — alcohol is the cause
Second episode of alcohol-induced pancreatitis = strong indication for alcohol cessation counseling
mustNotMiss: Necrotizing pancreatitis (worsening despite treatment — repeat CT at 72 hours if not improving)
Organ failure (monitor for ARDS, AKI, cardiovascular failure — Revised Atlanta criteria)
Infected necrosis (fever + worsening after initial improvement at day 7-10)
commonPitfalls: Keeping patient NPO for days — early enteral nutrition improves outcomes
Over-ordering lipase — don't trend it to determine when to feed (use clinical improvement instead)
Under-resuscitating — these patients lose massive third-space fluid
Over-resuscitating — can worsen edema and respiratory status. Use goal-directed targets.
Not addressing alcohol use — two episodes = chronic pancreatitis risk without intervention
teaching.attendingQuestions: The ED is giving you a clear diagnosis here. Your job is to assess severity, plan resuscitation, and anticipate complications. What scoring system helps you predict severity? What's your fluid strategy, and how will you know it's working?
